disease.name: Gastric Antral Vascular Ectasia (GAVE)
disease.note: GAVE or 'watermelon stomach' - vascular ectasia in gastric antrum with characteristic endoscopic appearance of longitudinal red stripes radiating from pylorus (like watermelon). Distinct from PHG (involves antrum vs fundus/body). Associated with cirrhosis, autoimmune disease, CREST syndrome.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Usually chronic low-grade bleeding causing iron deficiency anemia; acute bleeding less common

investigations.goldStandard: EGD showing longitudinal erythematous stripes of ectatic vessels radiating from pylorus in gastric antrum (watermelon stomach)

disease.pathophysiology: Unknown mechanism but associated with cirrhosis and autoimmune disease; ectatic mucosal blood vessels in gastric antrum cause chronic luminal bleeding from fragile vessels leading to iron deficiency anemia. Theories include mechanical stress from antral peristalsis, vasoactive substances (hypergastrinemia), and hormonal factors.

diagnosticClues.clue: Watermelon stomach appearance on EGD - erythematous stripes in antrum radiating from pylorus
Chronic iron deficiency anemia with no other identified bleeding source
Association with CREST syndrome or cirrhosis
Lesions limited to gastric antrum sparing fundus and body

exclusionClues.clue: Mosaic pattern in fundus/body suggests PHG, not GAVE
GAVE does NOT respond to portal pressure reduction (beta blockers, TIPS)
Focal gastric ulcer is not striped pattern
Diffuse gastritis lacks characteristic watermelon stripes
